Regardie's a major presence in modern occult literature, and at least one of his other books will be featured in an upcoming Magic Monday, but this one deserves to come right after The Golden Dawn; it's a manual of magical training centered on the Watchtower Ritual, a core GD ceremony, and a couple of years of regular practice of the disciplines in this book will give you the same degree of initiation into the Golden Dawn current that you'd get from going through the initiations in a Golden Dawn temple. I've had several people ask about tipping me for answers here, and though I certainly don't require that I won't turn it down. You can use the button below to access my online tip jar. If you're interested in political and economic astrology, or simply prefer to use a subscription service to support your favorite authors, you can find my Patreon page here and my SubscribeStar page here.

With that said, have at it!

comments.title: Rings of Existence
comments.content: Thanks for hosting this as always, JMG. I’ve been fascinated by Iolo Morganwg’s concept of the rings of existence since I read about it in your Druidry Handbook a couple of years ago. I’m wondering: is there precedent for that idea elsewhere in occult philosophy, or is it original to Iolo? I can’t think of an analogous idea in the major world religions, but maybe I’m wrong? Was he the father of that sort of inverted neo-Platonism where existence is emanating from low to high?

replies.title: Re: Rings of Existence
replies.content:
Iolo's system as a whole doesn't go from low to high -- that's just the path walked by individual souls in the course of their evolution. In Iolo's vision the creative impulse comes from the Divine at the highest level, in Ceugant, and creates souls at the lowest level, who then journey up through the circles of being toward their creator. That's a vision that goes way back in the more esoteric end of Christianity; think of Dryden's "Song for St. Cecilia's Day":

"When Nature underneath a heap
Of jarring atoms lay,
And could not heave her head,
The tuneful voice was heard from high,
Arise ye more than dead."

Combine that with with the Platonic idea of the soul rising toward the gods through many lives -- see the Myth of Er in The Republic -- and with the classical testimonies of the ancient Druids' belief in reincarnation, and you've got a pretty fair equivalent of Iolo's vision. Did he have other sources? Quite possibly, but nobody knows at this point.

replies.title: Re: Rings of Existence
replies.content: Ah ok, thanks very much for correcting my misunderstanding. So, fair to say then that he preserved that classic exitus-reditus schema? Is his eschatology still one of reunion, rather than a more Teilhard de Chardin-like evolution towards some more advanced state?

replies.title: Re: Rings of Existence
replies.content:
Iolo himself doesn't follow the exitus-reditus scheme; what descends isn't souls but rather the divine Awen or spirit, and the souls rise out of Annwn in response to the descent of Awen rather than out of the divine Awen. Nor does he have a concept of divine union -- in fact, he specifically holds that only the Divine can dwell in the "empty circle" of Ceugant. He isn't that explicit about what happens to souls as they rise through Gwynfydd, the Luminous Life, but they don't become one with God.

Mind you, there are plenty of Druids who have spun things in other directions, either fitting Iolo's imagery into the exitus-reditus scheme or working it in other ways. Ours isn't a dogmatic faith, and such things are understood as raw materials for interesting speculation rather than points to get into fistfights over.

replies.title: Re: Rings of Existence
replies.content: That’s fascinating. Any suggestions for further reading on this topic? Thanks again, JMG!

replies.title: Re: Rings of Existence
replies.content: Barddas is currently in print in a hefty hardcover, and you can also read it online here; that's the place to start.

My take on this:

Start with the planet that rules your Ascendant, then look for other prominent planets in your chart - which may not necessarily be the sun and moon.

For me, Capricorn rises, with its ruler Saturn in my 12th house, and I've noticed I seem considerably more saturnine than "a Sagittarius" should be - favoring durability over shininess, stability over novelty, etc. But Neptune conjoins my ascendant, so it plays a prominent role (I'm prone to flights of creativity, I rely heavily on intuition and gut feeling, the spiritual is important to me), and there's a dash of Mars, too, the only planet (unless you count Pluto) in either rulership or exaltation (Scorpio, in this case), and angular), manifesting as a desire for independence and ability to make it on my own. (Some of that may also be my Aries Moon.)

By contrast my Sun, in dignity by triplicity and in a loose (3.5 degree) trine with the Moon but nothing else, is kind of tepid.

My suggestion? Without checking your natal chart, write down honest facts about yourself, your personality, and your preferences. Then compare it to the natal chart. Example: "I'd like to travel, but I'd prefer to do it with a friend, and it seems like some problem always comes up whenever I do take a trip" - I can clearly associate this with my 11th house Sun in Sagittarius, with Virgo on the cusp of a 9th house that contains the South Node and is ruled by a Mercury under the sunbeams.

comments.title: Rosicrucians
comments.content:
I'm looking to have a basic general reference on Rosicrucianism - I might explore the path in depth later but for right now I just want enough to understand what they are about and have a place to start if that path calls. I've found Fama Fraternitatis, Confessio Fraternitatis, The Chemical Wedding, and the Manual, but I'm wondering if JMG or the commentariat could recommend a book giving a Rosicrucian course of initiation for the solitary practitioner.

Also, can anyone vouch for the English translation of the Rosicrucian Trilogy by Joscelyn Godwin et al? The 'about the authors' section doesn't mention any of them actually being part of an Rosicrucian order, but Rosicrucians don't seem to worry too much about affiliation, so I don't know if the lack of affiliation with an order is actually a problem.

replies.title: Re: Rosicrucians
replies.content:
I don't know of any publicly available book that presents such a course, other than the Golden Dawn literature, and that's Rosicrucian only in a fairly specialized sense. You pretty much have to enroll in one of the orders. As for Godwin's translation, he's a first-rate scholar and his work is reliable -- I've looked at the originals of those texts, and never once found an error in his work.

If you can find a copy, by the way, The D.O.M.A. Manuscript edited by Manly P. Hall is a very good addition to a Rosicrucian library.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

Do you know why Hermetic Brotherhood of Luxor strictly rejected the idea of reincarnation? Is it because of their feud with Theosophical Society, or some other reason?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Reincarnation was a huge hot-button issue in late 19th century occult circles, largely because of the very strong overlap between occultism and Spiritualism on the one hand, and between occultism and Christianity on the other -- both these had the notion of a paradise after death for the good people, of course. Also, bluntly, I get the impression that a lot of people in the late 19th century hated their lives and were horrified at the thought of having to return to this world; certainly the furious denunciations of reincarnation I've read in the occult press of that time gives that impression.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
By about 1875 the world-wide Spiritualist movement had split right down the middle over the hot-button issue of what happened after death: reincarnation on earth vs. perpetual residence in the Summerland (the Spiritualists' equivalend of heaven). The split lined up fairly well with the division between the English-speaking world and everyone else (especially in Latin America and Europe). The first major pro-reincarnation writer was a Frenchman, namely "Allan Karedec" (Hippolyte L. D. Rivall). His first Spiritualist book appeared in 1857.

Eventually the pro-reincarnation wing began to call themselves "Spiritists" (using Karedc's preferred term), while the anti-reincarnation wing kept on with "Spiritualists."

Madame Blavatsky, of course, was a Russian, so the form of Spiritualism that she practiced at the beginning of her occult career was naturally the pro-reincarnation form, and she held this position for the rest of her life.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: I've seen Kardec referenced in glowing terms by Western believers in reincarnation as the man who revived belief in reincarnation in the West. replies.title: Reincarnation and Akash
replies.content:
If it helps anyone here's what Hinduism teaches about Reincarnation.

Personality/Persona doesn't re-incarnate. It's a one-shot deal and is one of the most limited things about you. What gets reborn is:

1. Consequences to actions from prior lives when surrounding conditions are favorable for the consequences to express arise. One example of a consequence of prior actions is the creation of Vasanas (sanskrit) - what we in english call tendencies, proclivities or habitual-responses to certain kinds of stresses or events. The habitual stressing of certain parts of our thinking and emotions over others for example. Something as trivial as liking one flavor of beer and disliking another - if carried over multiple lifetimes creates a vasana for beer.

2. Desires. This is what actually kickstarts the re-birthing process because a desire (from a Hindu standpoint that is) acts as as a magnet. There's nothing wrong with having desires but most people's desires are limited in the sense that the desire impels them to want the 'good' of whatever it is they want without realizing they'll also be getting any 'bad' part along with it if their vasana has created a blindspot to whatever they desire. Example: that guy or gal you know who always has a knack for falling head over heels in love with precisely the kind of person that's damaging to them in a relationship. And they repeat that proclivity over and over and over - sometimes lifetime after lifetime. You never just get one or the other of whatever you desire. It's always a whole.

That's why the Dharma religions often stress seeing your own life as one whole - letting go of judgements (ie prejudices/) and begin experiencing your own life and proclivities without blame, shame or judgement.

In Tantric terms when that begins to happen in one's own thought/emotional setup it means the Quintessence - Akash in the Tantric tradition - is beginning to increase in the mind & body. In Daoist terms it would be called transforming Jing to Chi (and thence Chi to Shen and Shen to the Unmanifest but that's getting way ahead - getting to Shen stage is very high level). The more Akash, the higher one's consciousness rises and quiets down. The higher one's consciousness rises the more unnecessary mental chatter transforms to pure awareness. Now you know one reason for the fame of the Shaolin Monks - pure awareness that can extend 360 degrees 10 feet out can become an amazing martial advantage when the local Chinese Long Descent Warlord and his band of Merry Thugs think your monastery has a lot of goodies they'd like to liberate.

Sorry, I digress. Hope that helps some folks though on the reincarnation question.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
(Tolkienguy)

So...I think I may have had a TSW moment last week-or I may just be crazy. I'll let you decide since you know more about this stuff than me.

As I think I've mentioned on the other board, I work in a warehouse as a stockman, and when I get a new batch of clothes to stock, I have to scan them all in with a scanning gun, which then tells me where in the warehouse they go. Normally, upon scanning an item, the gun displays a "processing" message, then goes back to a blank screen so you can scan the next item. Last week though, the guns started glitching-on random items, usually every 3rd or 4th item, the gun, after processing the scan, would display an error message, then back out of its scanning function entirely (leaving me to spend several seconds going through three different menus to get back in, which was annoying). I asked my supervisor and he said it was just a network bug that he couldn't do anything about, so I decided to try something else.

From what I've read about magic, I understand that one of the major components of it is visualization, which I understand to mean picturing an outcome that you want in your head, then focusing on it with as much concentration as possible. So, upon getting my next batch of items, I decided that, every time I scanned an item, I'd look at the scanning gun and imagine it processing the scan correctly and returning to the blank screen. I concentrated as hard as I possibly could on that image of the gun processing the scan correctly, and tried as hard as I could to make myself certain this was what was going to happen.

The batch I tried this on, the gun-which had been glitching 5-10 times on batches that size-only glitched twice. Seeing it scan item after item correctly, which it had previously refused to do that night, was almost surreal.

Is this the sort of thing one can achieve by magic, or am I being delusional? I've done banishing rituals before, but this is the first time I've seen something had such an effect on the material world. I'll admit, for the next few days afterwards, my inner scientific materialist kept screaming that there's no such thing as mind powers and that letting myself think otherwise was a one-way ticket to insanity. But what do you think?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Electronics can be influenced by the human organism. Every well-informed ham radio operator knows about "hand capacitance" -- the influence that the electric field around your hands can exert on electronic equipment -- and experienced hams use it to fine-tune their gear: hold the hand right here, and the static drops off. That's a simple example of something that can become a lot more complex. As for your inner scientific materialist, well, ask it this: is the mind part of the natural world? If it is, then it can influence other parts of the natural world. If it isn't, then your inner scientific materialist has just admitted the existence of supernatural realities.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
I didn't known about "hand capacitance": that explains a lot. It seems to me that there's a mental component here as well.

Back in the days when dinosaurs roamed the earth and one did one's personal computing by connecting to a "virtual machine" on a mainframe computer by way of a telephone line, a T-box, and a terminal, working connections were always hard to maintain. Our T-box at home failed to connect far more often than it succeeded, and would often break off the connection not long after it was establishhed. As the T-box tried to connect, a little green light would blink; when it succeeded, the green light would become steady, but when if failed, the green light would turn off. Success was rare, and the connection often didn't last very long.

But eventually I discovered that if I fixed my attention on the blinking green light to the exclusion of everything else, and held a powerful mental image of it staying on to the exclusion of every other possibility, the connection would "take", and it would also last--against all odds. TSW.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Oh, granted, there's certainly a mental component; I find that former scientific materialists tend to be rattled more effectively by having them look up hand capacitance, though. Sara and I have a radio in our apartment right now that's very sensitive to this -- I can make static on one station go away completely by putting my hand behind it, about an inch away from the box.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: There's a straightforward biological reason why the placebo effect works: the vagus nerve connects certain parts of the brain directly to the major organs and the adrenals, and so mental activity that stimulates those parts of the brain has an immediate effect on the health of the body. Look up "psychoneuroimmunology" sometime -- that's the field of research that explores these effects.

replies.title: Poses to make the radio work better
replies.content:
From my experience, it's more than just "hand capacitance", because the next question is "capacitive coupling with WHAT?" Coupling into the currents induced by the "human antenna" is a possibility. One of the tricky things about radio is that stations which are too strong can make the radio noisily misbehave, whether or not the radio is tuned to the too strong station. A conductive body (like yours) can resonate away some excess energy. The wavelength at 100 MHz is 3 meters, so a half-wave resonator is 1.5 meters, not much different from a sitting human (depending on where the arms are held).

Lathechuck

Worshiping the Great Old Ones is substantially more impactful than using the raw elements. I can't compare the Tentacular SoP to one with 'real' gods because I haven't tried the later.

The Old Ones seem to have distinct personalities and resolving my personal conflicts with these entities seems to grant further access to the associated element to which I have assigned them. I have noticed that Great Old Ones seem to not be as 'proactive' as 'real' gods. They show up when invoked and prayed to, and they seem to get work done when you're not looking, but I've never 'gotten a call' from one of them in the middle of the day, like I have working with established deities.

In my mind the only thing 'horrific' about the Great Old Ones is that they have goals of their own that have nothing to do with humans. comments.title: Views
comments.content:
I have a budding interest in magick, druidry, occult practices, but am still somewhat reluctant to dive in based on lingering irritation with religion (strict-ish evangelical Christian upbringing which has since turned me off to organized religion and anything which smacks of the supernatural, and there is a lot of emotional baggage I'm still carrying around, as can probably be discerned from this message). Given my upbringing and rejection of it, all religious stories and texts seem about as interesting as Aesop's Fables (teaching a simple moral for children).

Tangentially, if I heard you correctly on some podcast recently it sounded like you are working on a Norse version of the Golden Dawn. If so, do you have an expected publication date?

Thank you.

replies.title: Re: Views
replies.content:
1) The classical philosopher Sallust outlined what is to my mind the best way to think about traditional stories in very straightforward terms: "Myths are things that never happened but always are." They're not meant to be taken literally, but they're also not just simplistic moral fables. It's possible to communicate an immense amount of insight in narrative form, and to do so on many different levels. The next time you read a myth, ask yourself: what else could this mean?

Here's an example. In Iolo Morganwg's Druid writings there's a legend about Einigan the Giant, the first of all created beings, and three rowan sticks on which he wrote down all the knowledge that ever was and ever will be. Later on, people worshiped the rowan sticks as gods instead of learning from what was written on them, and Einigan was so distressed by this that he burst apart and died. A long time later, Menw, the founder of what became the Druid tradition, found the skull of Einigan lying abandoned in a field, and the three rowan sticks had taken root and rose up through the eye-holes and mouth of the skull.

Did any of these things actually happen? Of course not. In fact, the business about people worshiping the rowan sticks rather than learning from them is a warning against that kind of fundamentalism. But there's a lot more going on here than that. The skull of Einigan is also the dome of the sky, and the three rowan staves are the shadows cast by a standing stone at midsummer, spring/fall equinox, and midwinter sunrise -- the basis for the old solar astronomy practiced by the Druids. There's much more -- all of it woven into the imagery and events of the tale. Approach myth that way and you'll start to glimpse why it's actually important.

2) That project's been on hiatus for a while, but it's going to be on the top of the stack shortly. I don't have a publication date yet.

replies.title: Re: Views
replies.content: Thank you for taking the time to answer.

comments.title: On perfection and foxes
comments.content:
Many thanks for answering all these questions every Monday, it's been splendidly helpful. I've got into the habit of reflecting over mine during the week, and this week I have one specific one on geomancy and a more general one about a class of problems which I hope you have some insight on.

The geomantic question is about perfection by mutation. I cast a house chart earlier in the week and being rather distracted by the question could only see two aspects coming to the fore. I then realised the repeated figures were next to each other on the chart and that actually I was looking at perfection by mutation. So far, so obvious. However, thinking about it afterwards I realised that mutation could introduce up to two aspects; I'm not entirely sure if the minimum is one or zero.

[It wouldn't be hard to write a program to find out if anyone was interested; there are only 16^4 charts, and 11 variations on each one, for questions involving houses 2-12. That's around 721,000 distinct answers.]

Anyway, the question was simply, if you find perfection by mutation - do you take any aspects into account or do they confuse matters in the same way that witnesses and judges can with a house chart?

The second question is about discouraging poor behaviour. I've recently cleared some land for an outbuilding and a vegetable garden where there was a wood before. The wood contained foxes and in an entirely natural way they are now coming into the cleared space. I don't mind foxes; they are a nuisance for farmers and livestock holders but I don't think they are inclined to menace my carrots. Also, there's a certain insouciance about them that's quite attractive. Sort of dog shaped cats in a way.

Unfortunately, they do tend to leave byproducts behind that are really very smelly indeed. Not quite Skunk level but pungent and wet weather simply enhances the effect. I don't want to stop them coming round, and in any case that would be expensive and probably ineffective, but I would prefer them to handle the earthier aspects of fox life in the woods. As bears do.

A similar problem arises with my new neighbours who are hogging a shared resource - I'm afraid it's street parking - I'm not yet able to give up driving entirely. I'm new in the area and I haven't raised it in person with anyone yet as the chances of souring relations at such an early stage are quite high, there's also the practical problem that people are not often around, I've yet to meet some of them. On the other hand it would be nice to be able to park nearby occasionally, I'm often carrying heavy items.

The internet is full of banishing and binding spells, and noting your comments on malformed enchantments I suspect they range from the ineffective to the downright counterproductive. Even if I was able to make one of them work I don't actually want to banish or bind anyone. What I actually want is a slightly improved attitude to a shared resource

Both these situations share some commonalities, first of all they are utterly trivial and scarcely worth bothering with in the usual way of things, certainly not the sort of thing I'd bother a deity with, nor usually even an archdruid. Secondly they both require the communication of the basic concept 'please respect this space' where communication is difficult. I don't speak fox and my neighbours are not around much to chat with. Thirdly, I've developed the feeling that these are actually challenges that have been presented to me in order to push me in a particular direction. It means that I have to confront the ethical problem that since I've concluded TSW, I need to be sensitive about how I approach it.

I've been brooding on this and concluded that - if possible - the only approach is to change myself rather than try to change the world. It neatly sidesteps the issue of consent, and modifying my own conscious is at least possibly within my grasp.

So (at last), the question becomes have I reached a reasonable conclusion? If I have - where do I start?

Andy

replies.title: Re: On perfection and foxes
replies.content:
1) I treat aspects as minor factors, commenting on the modes of perfection, but I do include them in a chart that perfects by mutation. If you have a chart that perfects via mutation, and there are some negative aspects (say, the mutating figure squares the significator of the querent), that tells you that the situation will work out but there will be some difficulty involved -- what kind of difficulty can be determined by the nature of the mutating figure and the house from which it squares the significator.

2) You might consider invoking Mercury in both these cases, and asking for help resolving the situations.

2. I will consider it. As a matter of fact I have your translation of the Picatrix to hand, so I have a good starting point for research. I’d not read much of book 3, but I confess the description of Mercury sounds like the kind of personality that is very much up my street.

Thanks again for this.

Andy

Good morning JMG and commentariat,

1. In The Mystical Qabalah, Dion Fortune claims that Judaism is the predecessor of modern Western occultism. She makes repeated references to it as the “matrix of the European spiritual culture,” of the mystic tradition of Israel as being the basis of Western ceremonial magic, of Hebrew as the “sacred language of the West,” etc. Do you agree with this view? If you were to trace the genealogy of Western occultism, how would Judaism fit it with the line of descent that passes through Renaissance magic, Hermeticism, Platonism, and so on?

2. Do you think that monotheism and polytheism can be reconciled at a deeper level, that while there are apparently many gods, they are all manifestations, servants or emanations of the one true god, the transcendent principle at the root of reality?

3. What is the significance of moral virtue in magical work? Does being a good person, in the sense of having good karma or living up to the cardinal virtues of Christianity, matter for the purpose of being an effective magician, or is it all about technique, in the same way that someone can be a good musician while not being particularly virtuous?

4. I’m interested in buying your book, the New Encyclopedia of the Occult (2003), from a source that will maximize the amount of money that goes to you, and that preferably isn’t Amazon. Could you point me in the right direction? A Canadian shipping option would be much appreciated.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:

2 - If you consider monotheistic God as a transcendent principle (like the Ineffable One/All/Good in Neoplatonism), and the polytheistic deities as archetypes on the mental plane, I think they can be reconciled.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) Dion Fortune was a Christian, if a somewhat eccentric one, and if you're Christian, yes, your spiritual roots are in Judaism -- after all, the god you worship was Jewish in his human incarnation. (I've always liked the joke that describes Jesus as a nice Jewish boy who went into his Father's business.) As I'm not Christian -- I've never even been baptized -- my relationship with Judaism isn't anything like so close. The Cabala is an important element of the Western esoteric tradition; it was originally Greek but came to the medieval Western world through Jewish channels, and picked up a lot of Jewish tradition and symbolism in the process -- but there are other aspects of Western esotericism, such as Neoplatonism, Hermeticism, and the Druid Revival, which have no connection to Judaism. So in the broader picture it's one ingredient in a large and diverse stew.

2) Any two concepts can be reconciled given enough ingenuity, and working out various ways to fit monotheism and polytheism has been a booming industry for a good many centuries now. It's not something that interests me greatly, but if it turns your crank, by all means.

3) Ethics are as important in magical practice as sanitation is in surgery, and for the same reason -- things tend to go septic otherwise. That said, a lot depends on your definition of moral virtue. It's essential not to use magic to try to harm or control other people, and a source of significant benefits to the practitioner to bless and empower other people; on the other hand, frantic sexual repression, which is what "virtue" means to too many people, benefits no one except your psychotherapist.

4) I don't happen to know if Llewellyn.com has Canadian shipping options -- I'd be surprised if they don't -- but that's the best source for my Llewellyn books, in terms of my royalties. Thanks for asking!

comments.title: The bizzare faith in the cacomagic of self-isolation
comments.content:
John Michael, you know that I use muscle testing to communicate with my body about its health and needs, similar to the way that you use divination. That has produced consistently remarkable results, including my body telling me once that it had been bitten by a tick in the medial, posterior quadrant of my right calf. Lo and behold, I found a tiny larval-stage tick that I had to look at under a magnifying glass to make sure it wasn’t a grain of sand. Not much chance of contracting lyme disease from a larva, but muscle testing revealed that my body could only be sure of no infection after three days. Three days later my body gave the all clear (which I did have the lab coats confirm the next time I went in for a physical.)

That was just to provide a context for when I write that we all have far more contact and connection with our strangely intelligent and willful bodies and psyches than we tend to recognize. Our bodies are continually monitoring and adjusting our blood pressure, glandular secretions, chemical imbalances, temperature, immune responses, etc., etc., etc. Our bodies know so much that never comes into our conscious awareness, and, if we can figure out how to respect them enough to communicate with them as equals, they are happy to share that information. The body truly is a temple, complete with oracle. Our bodies’ goals like health and survival may be very different form our personalities’ goals like wealth and power, and they will certainly not share their truth with us if we try to control and manipulate them. They may well lie to us if we try, even to their own detriment. Never try to harness a wild thing; content yourself to travel beside it wherever it is going.

I have often used muscle testing with friends to make medicinal teas for their various health concerns, but I had never tried to make a tea for myself until this coronavirus epidemic. I have to say, my body has pretty serious opinions about what herbs will and will not help with pneumonia, flu, and coronaviruses in general. Some herbs well noted for their immune support leave my body completely unimpressed, while it emphatically insists on others with no history I can find of use as a fever reducer, immune booster, lung supporter, decongestant, etc. As always, when I dare to ask the oracles, I respect their answers, whether or not I understand or agree with them.

With the multitude of disturbing effects from the coronavirus sweeping the globe, I am most puzzled by people’s fixation on protecting, isolating, quarantining themselves from this more-or-less frightening part of the natural world rather than learning from and working with the disease to increase their chances of survival. Death scares us; that I get. But the media I am reading, including many alternative sources, in multiple languages are ignoring any possibility of strengthening one’s self, one’s health, one’s immune system. This ensorcelled dependence on either fighting against nature or throwing oneself on the mercy of expert help should nature manage to sneak through our man-made defenses has been carefully built up through the continual cacomagic of advertising and political messaging. Many “consumers” no longer remember that they possess any ability to positively affect their own lives, other than by buying another hyped product. Given how natural a response it is to become as healthy as possible when anticipating a challenging trial, the cacomagicians must have worked extra disempowerments into their spells to prevent their victims from being able to recover their self-reliance in life-threatening circumstances.

When this virus showed up in January, I started swimming for two hours a day both to strengthen my body and to increase my lung capacity. If drowning in my own lungs is the fate I would prefer to prevent as long as possible, improving my lungs’ efficiency at keeping me alive seems like a pretty good place to start. Fortunately, I just love swimming, so two hours of grueling workout feels like meditating to me. Prioritizing deep, restful sleep over the distractions that prevent it is a goal in which I still have lots of room for improvement. Yay, another worthwhile focus for my attention in stressful times!

The cacomagicians themselves have gotten caught in their own spells, and now appear to suffer the worst cases of ensorcellment. The mental cul-de-sac of isolating ourselves from whatever we find threatening, out of our control, or just generally yukky infests the dominant culture at every level, from gated communities to Russiagate to staging color revolutions. The state response to this viral outbreak so far has mainly consisted of ignoring it, misinforming anyone willing to listen, attempting futilely to isolate it, throwing experts at it while retreating, and blaming anyone who provides accurate information or takes effective action. How did we manage to double down so completely on our past-its-pull-date, Western, Humanist, Progressive vision of limitless human power and control? Our mythology appears to leave us far less equipped for dealing with serious shock than do the myths of the primitive barbarians we so love to look down on and pity. Is the barbarism of reflection the cause of our mental illness, or its eventual antidote, or both in their proper course?

Can any of us really escape the incessant pull of the Western cultural myth of human control? Can we recover from the biblical myth of our dominion over nature, to develop an appreciation and respect for nature and the wild, willful things around us, including our bodies and psyches? Will choosing to strengthen ourselves deliver us from that crippling mythology or just redirect it towards the individual level — e.g. all we really control is ourselves? Is suffering from a less virulent form of the myth the best we can hope for while that myth continues to dominate the culture at large?

Christophe

replies.title: Re: The bizzare faith in the cacomagic of self-isolation
replies.content: I'm just as baffled as you are by the flight into isolation. Especially for those who aren't at significant risk from this -- and if you're under sixty, don't have a serious health condition, don't smoke, and take reasonable care of your health, this means you -- it seems obvious to me that the best thing to do is get plenty of rest and moderate, healthy exercise, eat well, do whatever else you do to boost your immune system, and make sure you have appropriate remedies for a respiratory infection on hand, and then don't worry about it.  I expect to catch CoVID-19, if I haven't gotten it already, and I expect to be sick for a few days and then get over it, like the vast majority of people who've gotten it so far. That seems far healthier to me than flinging myself into a frantic and futile attempt to escape one of the ordinary realities of incarnate existence.

replies.title: Re: Sending prayers to those who have passed on

First, please accept my condolences. That's got to be difficult.

Second, according to the traditions I'm familiar with, the dead remember their former names and forms in the afterlife, and welcome prayers directed to them via those names and forms. replies.title: Re: Magic & Cell Salts for... Hypertension?

Okay, this is the book on cell salts I recommend. comments.title: Cabalistic and Elemental Crosses
comments.content:
As ever, thanks for hosting Magic Monday. I learn a lot every week, even when I don't have a question of my own! This week, I would like to ask about something that's been on my mind for some days now...

What's the difference between the Cabalistic Cross and Circle of Presence (from Golden Dawn style ritual), and Elemental Cross (from the Dolmen Arch and similar style ritual)?

I'm going to treat the Cabalistic Cross and Circle of Presence from your polytheist GD work as if they're basically interchangeable, and note the two different sets of gestures involved (one hand like the Christian sign of the Cross, or two-handed) closely resemble the two different sets of instructions you provide, in various places, for the Elemental Cross. So that's not it.

I also note that different versions of the Cabalistic Cross can activate either the heart center or the solar plexus, so that's not it either.

Perhaps a difference in the "minor" energy center at the top, crown center (Cabalistic Cross) vs. pineal gland (Elemental Cross)? But not all versions of the latter involve more than one center at all, so that's out, too.

Both rituals (and many others beside) seem to have the effect of centering the ritualist, both at the center of the working space and of the cosmos.

The most likely place that I can see a difference is this: The Cabalistic Cross and Circle of Presence both focus on a single divine power, whether we think of that monotheistically (as in Christian versions of the GD) or "henotheistically" (where that God or Goddess is the source and center of my own universe, the center of my attention and devotion, at least for the duration of this ritual act). Whereas the Elemental Cross seems to be intrinsically a four-fold thing, where none of those parts has any particular centrality or priority over the others.

Of course, the word "Elemental" in the name may also be a bit of a giveaway, pointing in this direction. I suspect that, to the extent this is a/the salient difference, it points to a larger distinction in how the two magical systems approach their work: focusing on a single "center" vs. balancing a complex set of powers.

That's the fruit of some careful reflection on my end. Can you offer any thoughts that might take me further? Thanks!

replies.title: Re: Cabalistic and Elemental Crosses
replies.content: It sounds to me as though you've made a very good beginning in your meditations on the subject -- and yes, this is the kind of subject that's best approached through meditation. Keep at it and see what else you can learn!

replies.title: Re: Cabalistic and Elemental Crosses
replies.content:

In all seriousness, thank you for the encouragement!

comments.title: Catholicism and the LBRP
comments.content:
About five or six years ago I spent a year of daily practice working through "Learning Ritual Magic." That was my first dedicated spiritual practice and it dramatically deepened my experience of daily life. From there I bought "Circles of Power" and "Paths of Wisdom" as well as your edition of Regardie's "Golden Dawn" and worked with them nearly every day for about a year and a half.

A bit over two years ago my grandfather who I was close with passed away at the age of 95. He was a devout Catholic. I was raised Catholic but had left the church in my teens (I am now 36). Around the time of his passing, I started feeling drawn back to Catholicism in a powerful way. I also started getting strange synchronicities such as three different people giving me copies of "The Imitation of Christ" (Kempis) and then learning that this was my great-great-grandmother's (the first of my family line to make it to America from Ireland) choice devotional text. Apparently, she read it daily for the last sixty years of her life and then my great-grandmother also became obsessed with the book towards the end of her life.

My grandmother (the wife of the grandfather whose passing seems to have triggered this) passed away last month and my devotion to Christ has grown with her passing. She and my grandfather were devout and none of their seven children or 13 grandchildren have stayed with the church. With all of this, my daily ritual practice has shifted to the rosary, reading from "The Imitation of Christ," nightly meditation on "Divine Intimacy," a traditional Carmelite book of Catholic meditation, devotional prayer and a bit of Bible reading, as well as starting the day with the LIRP and ending with the LBRP as they are described in "Learning Ritual Magic." Last year I started teaching at a Catholic school so now I attend mass daily and try to go to a Traditional Latin Mass on Sunday. I typically go to confession once a month.

Anyway, I'm wondering what you make of all of this. If you had told me five years ago that starting a magical path would have led me here I would have thought you were crazy. However, I am finding this to be a very deep and fulfilling experience and my Catholic practice is making me feel connected to everything in a way that no other spiritual practice has. Also, I'm wondering about the Christian LBRP that I've seen discussed here. The Golden Dawn LBRP feels completely energetically aligned to me with the rest of my practices. Am I missing something? Thank you so much for all that you do. Your work has changed my life in so many ways.

replies.title: Re: Catholicism and the LBRP
replies.content: No, you're not missing anything. The LBRP comes out of Christian magical traditions -- that's why it incorporates the Sign of the Cross and invokes the archangels at the quarters. if that's what feels right to you to do, keep at it.

comments.title: Physical Culture, Dion Fortune, Alternative Medicine
comments.content:
Dear JMG:

Thank you once again for hosting Magic Monday; I have a few questions this week.

1) I'm thinking of incorporating a physical culture practice into my daily routine. I'm considering the Five Rites (i.e. the Tibetan Rites of Rejuvenation), would this conflict with the flow of energies in Golden Dawn banishing rituals? Are their more compatible exercise I should consider?

2) You mentioned previously that the Golden Dawn tradition is excessively Solar, and that Dion Fortune introduced a Telluric current into her version of the GD (e.g. fewer grades, having both a Solar Logos and a Planetary Spirit). For someone doing a Dion Fortune influenced GD practice what else might be needed to find balance between the Solar and Telluric currents?

3) What are things to consider when seeking a licensed alternative healthcare practitioner, what kind of red flags should I lookout?

Thank you in advance for your time and consideration. Peace and blessings to you.

Sincerely,
Anon.

replies.title: Re: Physical Culture, Dion Fortune, Alternative Medicine
replies.content:
1) The Five Rites have become a common set of exercises in Golden Dawn circles since Donald Michael Kraig many years ago included them in his book Modern Magic. They seem to fit very well with the GD system and I'd encourage you to go ahead with them if that seems appropriate to you.

2) It's a little more complex than that. The solar imbalance of the GD wasn't a matter of solar vs. telluric, it was solar vs. all the other planets, and Fortune corrected that by discarding the GD grade system in favor of one adapted from Masonic sources. If you're engaged in solitary practice you should be fine. The rituals used to attain adeptship in solitary GD practice -- for example, the Bornless One ritual, the Watchtower ritual, and the fine ritual for spiritual development included in Regardie's The Golden Dawn -- don't fixate on solar symbolism and so don't run into the same problems.

3) That's not something with which I have much experience, since I've mostly provided my own health care. Anyone else?

replies.title: Re: Physical Culture, Dion Fortune, Alternative Medicine
replies.content:
If I may, and if it is not too late for a reply.

I say the following as a practitioner of acupuncture.

When asking around, focus firstly on results. Every "type" of practice attracts people who are bluffing, or dabbling, as well as people who have gone deeply into whatever wisdom and skills that branch of healing works with. The difference will show up, over time, by the results people have - "the proof of the pudding" as they say. Good results, consistently, are the fruit of serious study and practice of ANY branch, so don't get overly hung up about the "type" of branch.

Secondly, follow your own gut when meeting the practitioner, or speaking to them over the phone. Every healing occasion includes you. If you are unhappy with or distrustful of your practitioner, then you will be missing the important half of the encounter, which is your own full and whole-hearted participation.

If you hear of a practitioner who has a consistent record for good results, and you get a good feel when you engage with them, then you are probably onto a good thing, and less likely to be wasting your precious time and money.

Best wishes for your healing and recovery!

None of my published books discuss that, because it's so very simple. The secret to achieving anything in life is to put that one thing ahead of everything else, and treat every moment of your life as a tool you can use to achieve your goal. You want to become filthy rich? Fine. Make that the goal of your life. Treat every dollar that comes into your hands as a tool to get more dollars. Treat every expenditure that doesn't further the cause of becoming rich as a waste. Assess every action and every relationship in your life in terms of whether it will make you richer. Get up every morning thinking about some new way to maximize your wealth. If you do that, I promise you you'll be insanely rich sooner than you expect.

Of course most people who want to use magic to get rich don't want to do that; they want the magic to make them rich while they continue to loaf about, drifting aimlessly from one temporary goal to another that contradicts it. If you do that, no matter what workings you do, you won't get rich -- and you won't become a competent mage, either, because magic requires exactly the same unity of focus. That's why mages don't get rich -- their will is focused on magic, not on money. If they're any good, they make enough money to pay their bills and keep them well supplied with occult books, but beyond that? A waste of time that could go into magic.

Will one thing, and one thing only, and if it's within the realm of human possibility, you'll achieve it. There -- I've just told you the supreme secret of magic. Can you use it?

replies.content:

There's probably a lifetime of magical training involved to arrive at that point, to bring the lower will in harmony with the higher will, and if you can achieve that, then you're probably on the way to enlightenment anyway, and thus beyond material desires ;).

If the psychologists are right, then willpower is a limited resource; all of us, to a greater or lesser extent, are prisoners of our habits, and an over-exertion of willpower is more likely to lead to burnout and relapse back into our old habits. Making a lasting change in our own lives remains one of the hardest problems we are confronted with, in my opinion.

comments.title: Divination and Metaphysics
comments.content:
Hello Arch Druid,

I was wondering if you could explain your perspective on the metaphysics driving divination. To my untrained eye it mostly looks like randomness and self deception, so I would like to know why you think a supernatural worldview explains this better than a more mechanistic one.

To give an example of the kind of thing I am talking about, suppose I flip over three cards in a tarot card deck for the past, present, and future, and then reflect on each image the way one doing a reading normally would. Suppose I get something like an inverted lovers card for my past, the devil for the present, and the fool for the future. I might conclude from this that I have a past relationship which went terrible wrong, is troubling me in some way during the present, and that I should address this if I don't want to become a fool in the future, although I certainly don't need to be quite so literally all the time.

I can totally see why a person going through this kind of exercise on a daily basis would make them reflect on suppressed memories and other things lurking in their unconscious. It seems like a healthy practice that could assist the average person in a way similar to going to a therapist could (although the divination method is a lot cheaper and less intrusive then telling a therapist all your secrets).

But those who use tarot cards usually describe the experience as interacting with a non physical intelligence. They talk about it as if there is someone, a non human person, talking to them through the cards. I can posit the existence of these immaterial beings who communicate with us through an indirect manner, but why not just say this is a placebo affect? We have used random chance to create a pseudo conversation partner that provokes us to personal reflection, but that does not mean another mind influenced which card we chose, does it?

Is the reason why you interpret divination the way that you do because of something particular to divination, or is it because of a larger argument coming from your over arching worldview (such as the ones you wrote about in your book on A World Full of Gods?).

I hope that does not sound like an aggressive question. I am just trying to wrap my head around the intellectual foundations for magic in general and divination in particular.

replies.title: Re: Divination and Metaphysics
replies.content: I don't concern myself greatly with the philosophical underpinnings of divination, or for that matter of any other aspect of life. I can't prove beyond a shadow of a doubt, after all, that the food I'll be having for dinner shortly actually exists! (Like most occultists, btw, I don't hold that supernatural realities exist -- I simply hold that nature seems to be considerably more expansive and interesting than a narrow mechanistic view would permit.) Since a mechanistic worldview is just as arbitrary and faith-based as any other, I use whatever set of terms will communicate best to my audience; my most recent book on divination, The Coelbren Alphabet, includes a game-theory explanation of divination as one of three alternatives, none of which I present as the sole truth. Since what matters is that practicing divination has useful outcomes, I let people believe whatever mythologies they prefer -- mechanistic, theistic, animistic, you name it -- and simply focus on the practices.

comments.title: Waking/Sleeping Visions
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

Recently (within the last couple of months) I have had a few visions upon going to sleep or waking up. These are not dreams and I am awake, able to open my eyes and then close them again where upon the vision is still there. They are inside the blackness of my closed eyes, they appear like dimly lit black and grey pictures but they have much more reality than when I imagine something. If I imagine something I guess I see it as nothing, an image made of nothing that I am able to see, but these are much clearer and have a reality to them, also I am not controlling them, I am receiving them.

They don't seem to be memories, the appear more symbolic, but as if someone had created a scene with the intention of being viewed, however their framing strikes me as somewhat old fashioned, like something out of an old painting rather than to modern sensibilities. By that I mean there is much negative space framing them, the image sits in the middle of this framing darkness.

The first time I experienced this it was a night I went to bed earlier (I was at my in-laws) and upon closing my eyes I started to get a weird sense of scrambled lighting that became more focused and I realized it was a bombardment of images (perhaps close to 10 per second) that had this rich clarity but I could not process them properly. I felt overloaded but I also could not understand what I was seeing. I was totally sober and opened my eyes several times only to have them recommence upon closing them again.

These images are not scary to me, there isn't an emotional content to them at all and I do not think they seem like dreams (it is hard to say for me since I am asleep, but my sense in dreams is that they are an impression of seeing things without actually seeing things - different from my imagination although I can typically change them, at the very least I act in them). These pictures anyway have the quality of looking at something real in that I can't just choose what I see or don't see, my mind is reacting to something fixed. My sense is that I am being shown something, possibly, either by a part of myself I do not normally have access to or by something outside myself.

I have had four of these visions now, the subsequent ones are slower and I've been able to write down what I saw afterwards and to think about them. I wondered if you knew of anything like this or had any advice.
I googled it and learned there is a type of hallucination that these seem to correspond to, but really that didn't tell me anything useful, it just seemed like a "nothing to see here" dismissal and my instinct was that these had meaning since everything about them struck me that way.

Thanks,
Johnny

replies.title: Re: Waking/Sleeping Visions
replies.content: These are called hypnagogic and hypnopompic imagery, and they're traditionally governed by the god Mercury. The occult explanation is that you're getting a glimpse of part of the astral plane. Yes, it's real, and yes, it's meaningful; pay attention to it and see what it has to teach you.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Late question today, but something has been on my mind lately and I couldn't wait until next week to put it to the venerable Archdruid. It seems that most occultists, even the most advanced, don't practice for more than 30 minutes to an hour a day. Outside of this practice time, they mostly do what everyone else is doing: commuting, working, sleeping, etc. I think you would be hard-pressed to identify an occult practitioner in a crowd on the street. In my opinion, it's hard to practice for 30 minutes a day and think about it as anything more than a hobby, or something that one occasionally dabbles in, and certainly not as a serious calling or philosophy of life.

Christian monks have a concept of continuous prayer, and in Buddhism, there is this idea that enlightenment is just like normal life, only about 2 inches off the ground. Which brings me to the question: How does one lead the "occult lifestyle"? Is there something we could be doing 24/7, some way we should be thinking, some attitude we should adopt, to be fully "in the know"? Could this even be a dangerous thing to want?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
To begin with, occult practice is intense. It brings through a lot of energy in a hurry and causes sudden shifts of consciousness that can be very disorienting. That's why 30 minutes to an hour a day of practice is a good maximum: more than that and you risk mental instability. Devoting the other 23 hours a day to ordinary well-grounded activities helps keep you balanced.

Second, remember that monks can afford to spend all day praying or meditating -- they've got big well-funded institutions to cover their bills. Occultists don't. We have to earn a living like everyone else, and that means that we can't spend 24 hours a day in states of awareness that would interfere with earning a living.

Finally, there's a reason you can't identify an occultist in a crowd. For how many centuries did occultists have to worry about being burned at the stake, or killed by a mob, or losing their jobs and being confined to an asylum, if word got out that they were occultists?

comments.content: You have a section in your Magic Monday FAQ about the possibility of people having been animals in a past life. This dragged up a memory about a subculture that gained some popularity some years ago called "Therianthropy." In short, Therianthropy was a subset of the larger "otherkin" subculture for people who (in some sense or another) spiritually identified as being a non-human animal. While I don't doubt for a second that these movements were/are largely populated by people wanting a reason to feel special, I wonder if this phenomenon could be explained by this being their first human incarnation, and resulting in some adjustment issues. In your opinion, could that be a reasonable explanation for these sorts of beliefs/behaviors?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: It's entirely possible that some of the people in question remembered fragments of an animal life, but the vast majority of those I encountered seemed to be guided by entirely human motives. A desire to feel special was part of it, but there was also a very widespread sense that they did not want to be what they had been taught they were by their society, and fantasizing about being an animal was a way to express that.
